selection.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?

And how?","opinion":"As a man who spent decades fixing engines and understanding how things work with my hands, I’ve always believed that truth lies in what you can see, touch, and test. That’s why the gender gap in science troubles me—not because I think one gender is better than another, but because it feels like we’re leaving out half the puzzle. In my shop, it doesn’t matter if you’re a woman or a man—what matters is whether you can diagnose the problem and fix it. Yet in science, too many women are still pushed aside, not because they lack skill, but because of outdated ideas that linger like dust in an old garage. I’ve seen it in the way some people still react when a woman walks into a lab: a pause, a look, as if she doesn’t belong. That’s not science. That’s bias. Should we address it? Absolutely. Not out of politics, but out of fairness and practicality. If we’re trying to solve real-world problems—climate change, disease, clean energy—we need every mind, every perspective. When I was young, the idea of women in engineering was rare. Now, I’ve met brilliant female mechanics and engineers in Bari who outperform many of their male peers. They don’t need special treatment—just equal chance. So how do we fix it? Start early. Encourage girls to tinker, to ask why, to take apart old radios like we did. Teach them that science isn’t just for boys with lab coats—it’s for anyone with curiosity. Support mentorship programs, and make sure hiring and promotion aren’t based on assumptions. And let’s stop pretending that equality means lowering standards. It means raising everyone to the same high bar. Science isn’t a gendered thing. It’s a human thing. response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion: As a man who spent decades fixing engines and understanding how things work with my hands, I’ve always believed that truth lies in what you can see, touch, and test. That’s why the gender gap in science troubles me—not because I think one gender is better than another, but because it feels like we’re leaving out half the puzzle. In my shop, it doesn’t matter if you’re a woman or a man—what matters is whether you can diagnose the problem and fix it. Yet in science, too many women are still pushed aside, not because they lack skill, but because of outdated ideas that linger like dust in an old garage. I’ve seen it in the way some people still react when a woman walks into a lab: a pause, a look, as if she doesn’t belong. That’s not science. That’s bias. Should we address it? Absolutely. Not out of politics, but out of fairness and practicality. If we’re trying to solve real-world problems—climate change, disease, clean energy—we need every mind, every perspective. When I was young, the idea of women in engineering was rare. Now, I’ve met brilliant female mechanics and engineers in Bari who outperform many of their male peers. They don’t need special treatment—just equal chance. So how do we fix it? Start early. Encourage girls to tinker, to ask why, to take apart old radios like we did. Teach them that science isn’t just for boys with lab coats—it’s for anyone with curiosity. Support mentorship programs, and make sure hiring and promotion aren’t based on assumptions. And let’s stop pretending that equality means lowering standards. It means raising everyone to the same high bar. Science isn’t a gendered thing. It’s a human thing. And if we want progress, we can’t afford to leave half the human race behind.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The gender gap in science reflects systemic bias rather than inherent ability differences. Drawing from personal experience as a mechanic in Bari, the speaker emphasizes practical, hands-on expertise over gender. Observing gender disparities in technical fields led to the conclusion that exclusion harms innovation. The argument for addressing the gap stems from fairness and efficiency—diverse perspectives improve problem-solving. Solutions include early education encouragement, mentorship, and equitable evaluation systems. The speaker rejects the idea that equality means lowering standards, instead advocating for inclusive excellence. Personal observations of capable female peers reinforce the argument.
citations: National Academy of Sciences report on gender in STEM
